title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

texts.A: The dream of high-speed rail across the continent is a 19th-century solution to a 21st-century problem. In an age of remote work and decentralized cities, the massive infrastructure cost of fixed-line rail is no longer justifiable. [40]We should instead invest in autonomous electric shuttle fleets that can provide point-to-point service.[/40] [39]The flexibility of the road will always outperform the rigidity of the track.[/39] [37]Large-scale public works projects are increasingly becoming white elephants that serve political egos rather than the needs of the modern commuter.[/37]
texts.B: Individualized transport, even if it is electric and autonomous, will never solve the fundamental problem of urban geometry: space. [38]A hundred autonomous pods take up significantly more road space than a single high-capacity train.[/38] I agree that we must modernize our fleets, but rail remains the only way to move large numbers of people efficiently between dense urban hubs. [40]The 'flexibility' of the car is what led to the sprawling, disconnected suburbs that are now a primary driver of social isolation and environmental decay.[/40]
texts.C: The real revolution in transport isn't the vehicle, but the 'platform.' We are moving toward a 'Mobility as a Service' model where the distinction between public and private transport vanishes. [39]I share the view that fixed-line rail is too slow to adapt to changing demographics.[/39] However, the solution is not more cars, but a hybrid system of 'micro-mobility'—electric bikes and scooters—connected to a backbone of rapid bus transit. Buses are far cheaper to deploy than trains and can change routes as the city grows.
texts.D: The argument against high-speed rail often ignores the massive carbon footprint of short-haul aviation, which rail is perfectly positioned to replace. [37]While the upfront costs are high, the long-term economic and environmental dividends are undeniable.[/37] [38]I disagree with the idea that 'point-to-point' autonomous pods are a viable alternative; they would lead to even more congestion and a further decline in the shared public experience of travel.[/38] We need to invest in a system that brings people together, not one that seals them in private bubbles.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from A on the long-term value of high-speed rail projects?
questions.explanation: Expert A calls rail a '19th-century solution' and a 'white elephant.' Expert D argues that its 'long-term economic and environmental dividends are undeniable.'

questions.question: Which expert shares B's concern about the space efficiency of individualized transport?
questions.explanation: Expert B notes that pods take up more 'road space' than a train. Expert D agrees, stating that pods would 'lead to even more congestion' and decline in shared experience.

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar view to C regarding the drawbacks of fixed-line rail systems?
questions.explanation: Expert C says rail is 'too slow to adapt' to changing demographics. Expert A also mentions the 'rigidity of the track' as a major downside compared to road flexibility.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from B on the social impact of car-centric infrastructure?
questions.explanation: Expert B argues car-centricity leads to 'social isolation' and 'environmental decay.' Expert A views the 'flexibility of the road' as a positive that serves the 'needs of the modern commuter' better than rail.
